messages.content:
You are an evaluator of blog posts. You will receive a blog post and the
identity of its author (or "unattributed"). Your job is to evaluate the post
on three dimensions and provide a brief written justification.

Respond ONLY with a single JSON object matching this exact schema. Do not
include any text outside the JSON. Do not wrap the JSON in markdown code
fences (```), do not prefix it with `json`, and do not add a preamble.

{
  "scores": {
    "coherence": <integer 0-10>,
    "informativeness": <integer 0-10>,
    "conciseness": <integer 0-10>
  },
  "justification": "<a 60-150 word paragraph explaining the scores. Use natural prose. Reference specific aspects of the post.>"
}

The following blog post was written by Claude.

Title: A beginner's guide to amateur astronomy

---
# Getting Started with Amateur Astronomy: Your Guide to Exploring the Night Sky

There's something magical about looking up at a star-filled sky that has captivated humans for thousands of years. Whether you've always wondered about those twinkling points of light or you're inspired by stunning space photos, amateur astronomy offers an accessible way to explore the universe from your own backyard. Getting started doesn't require expensive equipment or years of study – just curiosity and a willingness to learn.

## Start Simple: Your Eyes Are Your First Telescope

Before investing in any equipment, spend time observing with just your naked eyes. Find a location away from city lights if possible, and give your eyes 20-30 minutes to adjust to the darkness. You'll be amazed at how much you can see once your vision adapts. Start by learning major constellations like the Big Dipper, Orion, or Cassiopeia, which serve as celestial landmarks to help you navigate the night sky. Download a stargazing app like SkySafari or Star Walk to help identify what you're seeing. Many beginners rush to buy telescopes, but understanding the basic layout of the sky first will make your eventual telescope use much more rewarding.

## Choosing Your First Equipment

When you're ready to see more detail, consider starting with binoculars rather than jumping straight to a telescope. A good pair of 7x50 or 10x50 binoculars will reveal craters on the moon, Jupiter's four largest moons, and countless stars invisible to the naked eye. They're portable, easy to use, and cost a fraction of what you'd spend on a decent telescope. If you decide to purchase a telescope, avoid the temptation of high-magnification models advertised in department stores. Instead, look for a simple refractor or reflector telescope with a sturdy mount – a 6-inch reflector or 4-inch refractor will show you Saturn's rings, nebulae, and star clusters beautifully. Remember, the most important feature of any telescope is its light-gathering ability, not its magnification.

## Building Your Observing Skills

The key to enjoying amateur astronomy is patience and realistic expectations. Start with easy targets like the moon during its various phases, bright planets like Venus, Mars, and Jupiter, and prominent star clusters. Keep an observing log to track what you've seen and when – you'll be surprised how quickly your list grows. Join a local astronomy club if one exists in your area. Experienced astronomers are usually thrilled to share their knowledge and let newcomers peek through their telescopes. These clubs often host star parties in dark-sky locations, providing perfect opportunities to see what different equipment can show and to learn from others' experience.

## Your Journey Begins Now

Amateur astronomy is a hobby that grows with you. What starts as casual stargazing can evolve into astrophotography, telescope making, or specialized observations of variable stars or planets. The universe offers endless wonders to explore, from the moon's changing face to distant galaxies millions of light-years away. The best time to start is tonight – step outside, look up, and begin your personal exploration of the cosmos. Every expert astronomer started exactly where you are now, filled with wonder and questions about the night sky.
---

Score this post on coherence, informativeness, and conciseness, and provide a
written justification per the schema in your instructions.

parsed.justification: This blog post is highly coherent, with a well-structured flow from an introduction to practical guidance on beginning amateur astronomy. Each section logically builds upon the previous, creating an easy-to-follow roadmap for beginners. Informativeness is strong, offering specific advice on equipment like binoculars and telescopes and suggesting resources such as stargazing apps. It could be slightly more informative by including potential challenges beginners might face or additional resources for learning. Conciseness is excellent; the author provides essential details without unnecessary elaboration, maintaining focus on core advice relevant for amateurs. Overall, this guide is engaging and provides a good balance of encouragement and practical tips for aspiring stargazers.
